key_insight: A physics-informed conditional diffusion model can forecast phase-field crack evolution while preserving temporal coherence and providing localized uncertainty estimates without changing the trained network.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical surrogate for expensive fracture FEM simulations, enabling faster long-horizon crack growth prediction with uncertainty maps to support engineering decisions.
method: Condition the DDPM on rolling damage history plus kinematic proxies (phase-field velocity and gradient magnitude).
Generate autoregressive full-field fracture states to predict crack initiation, propagation, and branching.
Use ensemble variance from stochastic sampling to localize uncertainty and flag likely error regions.
evidence_signal: The model achieved 0.12 px mean crack-tip error on held-out cases, 90% precision for high-variance error detection, and maintained Dice = 0.929 ± 0.010 over 50-step rollout versus 0.423 for a deterministic U-Net.
watch_item: Inference is still relatively costly for a surrogate (~3.6 s per step), so deployment will need careful tradeoffs between uncertainty quality, rollout length, and runtime.
paper_title: Uncertainty-Aware Crack Growth Forecasting via Conditional Denoising Diffusion Models for Phase-Field Fracture
